claim.statement: Learning traps represent fundamental limits on learning accuracy that arise when the interaction between exploration, generalization, and selective attention causes learners to form persistent false beliefs even in information-rich environments.
claim.statement_location: Abstract / Introduction, first page (summary of main contribution)

data.source:
1) Human participant data collected via Amazon Mechanical Turk
2) Computer simulation output from the ALCOVE-RL model
data.sample_size:
Experiment 1: 101 participants (≈50 per condition)
Experiment 2: 400 participants
Simulations: thousands of model runs (exact count varies by condition)
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual participant in behavioral experiments; individual model run in simulations
data.notes: Data include both behavioral choices and simulated learning trajectories.

method.description: The authors combined large-scale computer simulations with two online behavioral experiments to examine how selective attention and feedback contingencies create ‘learning traps’. They adapted the ALCOVE category-learning model for reinforcement learning (ALCOVE-RL), simulated performance under five feedback/attention conditions, and then tested analogous conditions with human subjects performing multidimensional decision tasks on Amazon Mechanical Turk. Outcomes (strategy adoption, acceptance rates, belief accuracy) were analyzed with Bayesian multilevel models.
steps: Adapt the supervised ALCOVE model to a reinforcement-learning version (ALCOVE-RL) capable of action-dependent feedback.
Specify five simulation conditions varying feedback informativeness and the presence of selective attention.
Run the model for multiple learning blocks and record the dimensions attended and payoffs earned.

Recruit human participants on Amazon Mechanical Turk and randomly assign them to feedback conditions (contingent vs. full information) and, in Experiment 2, to intervention conditions (individuation, occlusion, noise).
Present a multidimensional classification/decision task (bees in Experiment 1; job applicants in Experiment 2) over several learning blocks plus a test block without learning.
Record choices, feedback received, and post-task judgments (e.g., perceived proportion of negative prospects, feature identification).
Compute 1-D and 2-D strategy scores for each participant or model run.
Analyze outcomes using Bayesian multilevel (hierarchical) models to estimate differences between conditions and assess evidence for learning traps.
method.models: ALCOVE-RL simulation model; Bayesian multilevel (hierarchical) statistical models
method.outcome_variable: Adoption rate of the correct two-dimensional strategy (2D score) and the incorrect one-dimensional strategy (1D score); acceptance/approach rate; accuracy of feature identification
method.independent_variables: Feedback condition (contingent vs. full information); selective attention capability; interventions (individuation, occlusion, noise); learning block
method.tools_software: Bayesian modelling software (e.g., Stan/R) implied; specific packages not stated

results.summary: Simulations showed that when feedback was contingent and selective attention was enabled, over a quarter of models fell into an attentional learning trap, whereas full feedback or removal of selective attention produced fast, accurate learning. In Experiment 1, human participants under contingent feedback relied more on the erroneous one-dimensional rule (mean 1D = .83) than those with full information (mean = .75), while full-information participants more often adopted the optimal two-dimensional rule (mean 2D = .82 vs. .72). Only 22.9 % of contingent-information participants correctly identified the relevant features compared with 40.4 % in the full-information condition. Interventions tested in simulations and Experiment 2 (individuation, occlusion, noise) modestly improved model performance but were largely ineffective for human learners, indicating the robustness of learning traps.

metadata.original_paper_title: The Limits of Learning: Exploration, Generalization, and the Development of Learning Traps
